content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

April 14, 1994

John J. Johnson
General Manager
Casitas Municipal Water District
1055 Ventura Avenue
Post Office Box 37
Oak View, CA 93022

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-94-050

Dear Mr. Johnson:

This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of Mike Frees regarding his responsibilities under the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").¹ Since your advice request does not refer to a specific
governmental decision, but instead seeks general guidance, we are
treating your request as one for informal assistance.²

QUESTIONS

1. Is it a conflict of interest for Director Frees to
concurrently serve on the Board of Directors of the Casitas
Municipal Water District and the Ojai Valley Sanitary District,
two public agencies with overlapping jurisdictions?

2. Should Director Frees disqualify himself from
participating in decisions where the interests of the two agencies
appear to be in conflict? For example, is it a conflict of
interest for Director Frees to participate in decisions concerning
the districts' contract to process wastes, or concerning the
construction of new plants, where each of the districts may be
competing for limited resources within the community to accomplish
these projects?

___________________________

¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-94-050
Page 2


3.  Under the Act, may Director Frees participate in the
Casitas District's decisions concerning his former union or his
retirement benefits?

CONCLUSIONS

1. and 2.  There is no conflict of interest under the
Political Reform Act for Director Frees to hold positions at two
different public agencies or to participate in decisions at one
agency which may conflict with the interests of the other agency.
However, other laws outside of our jurisdiction may apply to your
question.  We recommend you contact the Attorney General's Office
or your County Counsel for the purpose of obtaining an opinion
with respect to the doctrine of "Incompatible offices" and other
conflicts-of-interest laws that might apply to Director Frees'
situation.

3.  Under the Act, Director Frees may participate in the
Casitas District's decisions concerning his retirement benefits
since retirement benefits are considered part of his former local
government salary, which is exempt from the definition of
"income."  As long as the union is not an economic interest of
Director Frees, he may participate in decisions concerning it.

FACTS

Several weeks before his retirement from the Casitas
Municipal Water District (the "Casitas District"), Mike Frees was
appointed as a director of Casitas Municipal Water District (the
"District") on December 8, 1993, a position for which he receives
compensation.

Director Frees also serves on the Ojai Valley Sanitary
District Board of Directors, a position for which he receives
compensation.  The Ojai Valley Sanitary District (the "Ojai"
District), created under the 1923 Sanitary District Act, is wholly
within the Casitas District's boundaries.

The Casitas District contracts with the Ojai District to
process wastes that it hauls to the Ojai District's treatment
facilities.  The value of the services rendered by the Ojai
District and paid for by the Casitas District is in excess of
$12,000 per year.

The Ojai District has considered a number of projects to
reuse waste water within the Casitas District's service area.  The
current status of that project is that it is not feasible due to
financing costs.  However, in the future an agreement between the
two districts may be reached for the sale of such water within the
boundaries of the Casitas District, since there are parts of the
law which prevent duplication of service.

Furthermore, the Ojai District is under an order to construct
a new treatment plant.  The Casitas District also is under an

Our File No. I-94-050
Page 3


order to build a new water filtration plant. Thus, both districts
may be in competition for resources within the community to
accomplish these expensive construction projects.

As a former employee of the Casitas District, Director Frees
was a member of a union which this year went to impasse with the
Casitas District over salary matters. It would appear from the
comments of the Casitas District Board of Directors that Director
Frees was selected to serve on its board, in pertinent part, to
help resolve the Casitas District's impasse with that particular
union. One of Director Frees' duties as a member of the Casitas
District Board of Directors is to participate in contractual
matters concerning his former union and other represented
employees.

Furthermore, as a retired employee, Director Frees receives a
retiree health care benefit from the Casitas District. The total
cost of this benefit in 1993 for approximately 23 retirees was
estimated to be in excess of $126,000. As a member of the board,
Director Frees would be involved in decisions concerning this
retiree health benefit.

ANALYSIS

Incompatible Offices

The Act does not prohibit a public official from holding
multiple public positions, either within a single agency or
different agencies. However, the conflict-of-interest provisions
of the Act (section 87100 et seq., discussed below) do prohibit a
public official from participating in a decision that will have a
financial effect on his or her economic interests. This
prohibition is applied on a decision-by-decision basis.

Please note that other laws do restrict the ability of public
officials to hold two different public offices simultaneously if
the offices are determined to be "incompatible offices." It would
be advisable to contact your County Counsel or the Attorney
General's Office with respect to the doctrine of "incompatible
offices" and other conflicts-of-interest laws that might apply to
Director Frees' situation.

Conflicts of Interest

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making,
participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest.

Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a
financial interest if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally, on the official or a
member of his or her immediate family or on:

Our File No. I-94-050
Page 4


     (a) Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

     (b) Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars (($1,000) or more.

     (c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

     (d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

     (e) Any donor of, or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
provided to, received by, or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.


     Applying the law to Director Frees' situation, we initially
note that under subdivision (d) of Section 87103 above, when an
official serves as a member of the board of directors for a
business entity, such service normally results in the business
entity becoming an economic interest of the official. However,
this result does not occur in Director Frees' case. This is
because Director Frees serves on the boards of directors of local
governmental agencies.

     Section 82005 defines "business entity" as an organization or
enterprise operated for profit, including but not limited to a
proprietorship, partnership, firm, business trust, joint venture,
syndicate, corporation or association. Since a local government
agency is not an organization or enterprise operated for profit,
it is not a "business entity" as defined by the Act. Thus,
Director Frees' membership on the board of directors of the
Casitas District and the Ojai District does not result in the
districts becoming economic interests of Director Frees.
(Williams Advice Letter, No. I-92-287.)

     You have informed us that Director Frees is compensated by
the districts for serving on the board of directors. Under
subdivision (c) of Section 87103 above, sources of income to an

Our File No. I-94-050
Page 5


official (whether the source is an individual or a for profit or
nonprofit entity) are economic interests of a public official. However, this result does not occur in Director Frees' case. This is because salary and reimbursement for expenses per diem received from a state, local or Federal government agency are not regarded as income for purposes of the Act. (Section 82030(b)(2).) Thus, since the Casitas District and the Ojai District are local governmental agencies and the income he receives from these agencies is in the form of compensation for his services, Director Frees may have an economic interest in the districts by virtue of this income.

     Absent some other disqualifying economic interest as set forth in Section 87103 above, or some direct financial effect on Director Frees personally resulting from a governmental decision, Director Frees does not have a conflict of interest because of his membership on the boards of directors of these districts. However, please note, as stated above, our advice is limited to the Political Reform Act.

Retirement Benefits

     As discussed above, Section 82030(b)(2) exempts salary or per diem received from a state, local, or federal government agency from the definition of "income." In its Moore Opinion (In re Moore (1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 33), the Commission held that pension benefits are income, specifically, a type of deferred salary:

     When an employee agrees to work for the county, he is entitled to certain benefits which comprise his compensation. He receives, for example, a monthly salary, vacation and sick leave and retirement benefits .... [A]s the California Supreme Court has observed, retirement benefits, such as a pension, "do not represent the beneficent (sic) gratuities of the employer; they are, rather, part of the consideration earned by the employee." A pension, therefore, is essentially a type of deferred salary whereby an employee agrees to receive a smaller payment while working in exchange for the security of receiving the remaining portion of his compensation after he retires.

     (In re Moore, supra at 36; citations and footnote omitted, emphasis added.)

     Thus, for purposes of the Act, pension benefits from a governmental agency are treated like salary from a governmental agency and are not considered income within the meaning of Section 82030(a).

Our File No. I-94-050
Page 6


     In addition, the Commission has provided that actions by
public officials, employees, or employee representatives relating
to their compensation or the terms or conditions of their
employment or contract do not generally constitute a conflict of
interest under the Act. (Regulation 18700(d)(3).)

     See also, Regulation 18702.1(c)(1) which provides that a
public official generally does not have to disqualify himself or
herself from a governmental decision if the decision only affects
the salary or per diem the official or his or her spouse receives
from a state or local government agency. (But see, Regulation
18702.1(c)(2).)

     Thus, since Director Frees' retirement benefits are
considered part of his former government salary from the Casitas
District, he may participate in decisions concerning those
benefits. (Hensley Advice Letter, No. A-92-525.)

     If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.³

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel

                                        [signature: Deanne Stone]
                                        By: Deanne Stone
                                        Senior Commission Counsel




_____________________________
     ³ Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.

2.

and 2.
